reasoning: Beto O'Rourke recently (late April 2025) signaled that he would consider running for the U.S. Senate in 2026 if "Texans want him to." He has been holding town halls and rallies across Texas through May 2025, which mirrors the behavior of a candidate in an exploratory phase. The 2026 midterms under a Trump presidency and a potential messy GOP primary between Senator John Cornyn and AG Ken Paxton present an excellent political environment for Texas Democrats. However, there are strong reasons for O'Rourke to delay any formal announcement until after July 1. Formally declaring candidacy subjects him to strict FEC fundraising limits, whereas he currently has more organizing freedom operating his "Powered by People" organization. Additionally, delaying allows him to evaluate Q2 fundraising reports from Republicans (due in mid-July) and to navigate potential intra-party competition if Rep. Colin Allred decides to run again. Given "Beto fatigue" from his three previous consecutive statewide/national losses and the strategic benefits of delaying a launch until late summer or fall, a formal announcement before the brief July 1 window closes remains plausible but is unlikely.
